actions.thought: I am on the Button with Ah7s, which is a strong starting hand in a heads-up situation. I want to take the initiative and build the pot early. A standard open-raise to 3 times the big blind is an effective way to put pressure on the Big Blind and define their range while retaining the advantage of position throughout the rest of the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with KTo against a Button raise to 300 (3bb). In heads-up play, KTo is a strong hand well above the median. I am getting 2:1 pot odds to call, needing roughly 33% equity. KTo has significantly more equity than this against a standard wide heads-up opening range. While I will be out of position post-flop, the hand is strong enough to defend by calling. A 3-bet is possible but calling keeps the opponent's range wide and controls the pot size.

I hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (Kd Th) on a dry, rainbow board (3h Tc 5s). In heads-up play, the Button's raising range is very wide, so my hand is extremely strong here. However, as the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out the opponent's air hands, whereas checking allows the opponent to make a continuation bet with a wide range, including bluffs and worse value hands. I intend to check-call if they bet to keep their range wide.

On a dry board of Tc 5s 3h, my Ace-high has significant showdown value but I want to continue the aggression after my pre-flop raise. A standard continuation bet of roughly 1/3 the pot (200 chips) is effective here to fold out hands that missed the flop, like KQ or KJ, and to define the Big Blind's range. If called, I have two overcards to the board and can potentially improve on the turn.

I hold Top Pair with a King kicker on a dry, unconnected board (3h Tc 5s). My opponent, the pre-flop aggressor, has bet 200 (1/3 pot). In heads-up play, this c-bet sizing is standard for a very wide range, including air, lower pairs, and draws. My hand is well ahead of this range. Raising now would likely fold out all of the opponent's bluffs, which I want to keep in the pot to extract value from on later streets. Since the board is not coordinated (no flush draws, limited straight draws), giving a free card is not dangerous. Therefore, the optimal play is to call to induce further action on the turn.

I hold Top Pair with a King kicker on a board of 3h Tc 5s 4s. The turn card (4s) introduces a spade flush draw and completes some straights (67, A2), though these are a small part of the opponent's wide button range. As the pre-flop caller out of position, my standard line is to check. Leading out (donking) here would likely fold out the opponent's bluffs and air, which I want to keep in the pot. By checking, I allow the opponent to continue telling their story—either by bluffing at the scare card, betting a worse value hand, or checking back. My hand is strong enough to call a bet, but not strong enough to build a massive pot out of position voluntarily against an uncapped range.

The turn 4s is a relatively neutral card, though it does open up some straight possibilities (like a 6 for a 3-4-5-6-7 straight). My Ace-high still has some showdown value against my opponent's potential missed draws or lower pocket pairs that might have called the flop. Since Pro checked, checking back allows me to control the pot and see a free river card. This avoids the risk of being check-raised off my hand and keeps the pot manageable while I still have a chance to improve or win at showdown.

The river is the 6c, making the final board 3h Tc 5s 4s 6c. This places a 4-card straight on the board (3-4-5-6). Any 7 or 2 makes a straight. While I hold Top Pair (Kings), the strength of my hand is significantly diminished by the runout. My opponent checked back the turn, which caps their range somewhat, making it less likely they have a strong value hand like a set or strong two-pair, but they could easily have a random 7 or 2 in their wide button range. Leading out here (donk betting) is ill-advised because worse hands will fold and better hands (straights) will raise or call. Checking allows me to try to get to showdown cheaply against hands like mid-pairs or Ace-high, or to bluff-catch if the opponent decides to stab at the pot with air.

The river 6c completes a 7-high straight (3-4-5-6-7) for me because I hold the 7s. In heads-up play, this is a very strong hand. The only realistic hand that beats me is exactly 7-8 (an 8-high straight), and I block that combination by holding a 7 myself. Since my opponent checked to me, I have the opportunity to bet for value. I expect to be called by a 10, any lower straight (containing a 2), or we will split the pot if my opponent also holds a 7. A bet of 600 into the 1000-chip pot is a strong value bet that can still be called by mediocre hands.

On the river, the board reads 3h Tc 5s 4s 6c, putting a four-card straight (3-4-5-6) on the board. Any 7 or 2 makes a straight. My opponent's line—checking back the turn and then betting 600 into 1000 on the river—is consistent with a draw that checked for a free card and got there, or a polarized bluff representing the straight. While I hold Top Pair (Kings), it is merely a bluff-catcher on this runout. In Hand 1 of the tournament without specific reads, assuming the opponent is aggressively bluffing a 4-to-a-straight board is risky. The turn check suggests they likely had a draw (like 7x or 2x) or a marginal hand that would check back the river. The bet signifies strength. Preserving chips is preferable to hero-calling against a range heavily weighted towards straights.

I am on the Button (SB) with A4o, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play due to the Ace blocker and high-card value. Folding is too tight. Limping is an option, but raising allows me to take the initiative, utilize my positional advantage, and apply pressure on the Big Blind. A standard min-raise to 200 is appropriate to deny equity to random hands and build the pot with a decent holding.

Holding 2d 5s in the Big Blind, I am facing a raise from the Button. While the pot odds are 3:1 to call (100 to win 300), 25 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Playing this hand out of position against a raise will lead to difficult post-flop situations where I am often dominated or forced to fold. I will fold here and wait for a more profitable spot.

Holding Kd 5s on the button in a heads-up scenario, I have a hand that is strong enough to open for a raise to put pressure on the big blind. King-high has decent showdown value and playability post-flop with position. I will raise to 300, which is 3x the big blind, to maintain an aggressive image and build the pot early.

I am holding 7s 3h in the Big Blind, facing a 3x raise to 300 from the Button. 7-3 offsuit is statistically one of the worst hands in poker. Even against a very wide opening range, my equity is likely around 30-32%. With the pot odds offered (calling 200 to win 400, or 2:1), I would need approximately 33% equity to call profitably, not accounting for the positional disadvantage and poor playability of the hand post-flop. Since this hand is at the very bottom of my range and the price is not compelling enough to defend, the only correct play is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with Q8 suited, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, well above the average hand strength. I have position on the opponent post-flop. Given the stack depths (roughly 95BB effective), a standard min-raise is appropriate to take the initiative, build the pot with a playable hand, and potentially steal the blinds immediately. Folding is too tight, and limping caps my range unnecessarily.

I have Ace-Deuce offsuit in the Big Blind. The opponent raised to 200 from the Small Blind/Button. I have already posted 100 as the Big Blind, so it costs me 100 more to call into a pot of 300. This gives me 3:1 pot odds. Ace-high hands have significant equity against a standard heads-up opening range, and the price to see a flop is very favorable. I will call to defend my blind.

I am in the Big Blind and first to act on the flop. The board is 9c-Ts-3c, which is fairly coordinated. I missed the flop with my Ace-Deuce offsuit, though I have the deuce of clubs for a very weak backdoor flush draw and one overcard. Leading into the pre-flop raiser (donk betting) on this board texture doesn't make much sense for my range. I will check and see how the opponent reacts.

I hold Q8s (diamonds) on a flop of 9c-Ts-3c. I raised pre-flop and the Big Blind called. The opponent has checked to me. This board texture (9-T with a flush draw) connects well with the Big Blind's calling range (pairs, straight draws, flush draws). While I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack) and one overcard, my hand is currently just Queen-high. Betting here risks getting check-raised, which would force me to fold my equity. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, potentially hit my straight or top pair on the turn, and keeps the pot manageable with a marginal hand.

I am out of position with Ace-high on a board that is becoming increasingly coordinated (9c-Ts-3c-Qs). My opponent checked back the flop, which could indicate a marginal made hand or a total miss. Ace-high has some showdown value, but it is not strong enough to bet for value, and betting as a bluff is risky given how many draws or pairs in the opponent's range might call. I will check to see the river for free and reassess.

I have improved to Top Pair (Queens) on the turn, and I also hold a gutshot straight draw (a Jack gives me 8-9-T-J-Q). The board (9c Ts 3c Qs) is extremely wet, featuring two flush draws (clubs and spades) and connected cards supporting straight draws. Since I checked back the flop, my hand is under-represented. I cannot afford to check back again and give a free card to the numerous draws in the opponent's range. I bet for value from worse pairs (Tx, 9x) and to deny equity/charge draws.

The turn Queen of Spades is a significant card that likely connects with the opponent's range, especially since they raised from the button pre-flop. My Ace-Deuce offsuit has no pair and very little potential to improve to the best hand on the river. While the opponent's check on the flop might indicate weakness, their 250 bet into a 400 pot on the turn is strong enough to make a call with just Ace-high unprofitable, especially being out of position. I will fold and preserve my stack.
I am on the button with Ah6h, which is a strong starting hand in a heads-up match. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the pot and leverage my positional advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d2d facing a 3bb (300) raise from the Button. The pot odds require roughly 33% equity to call (200 to win 600). 82s is a very weak hand, even heads-up, with equity hovering around 30-33% against a wide opening range. Furthermore, playing this hand out of position against a 3x raise is difficult and likely to realize less than its raw equity. Given the larger raise size and the poor playability of the hand, folding is the standard and correct decision to preserve chips.

In heads-up play, J7s is a playable hand from the button, well within the top 80% of starting hands. The opponent has previously folded to a min-raise from the Big Blind (Hand #2), making a steal attempt profitable. I will raise to 200 to take the initiative and play the pot in position.

I am in the Big Blind holding K7o. Pro has raised to 200 from the Button. I need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 pot odds. K7o is well above the threshold for a defending range in heads-up play, as it has significant equity against a wide button opening range. I will call to see the flop and play the hand from there.

I have King-high on an Ace-high board after calling a pre-flop raise. This board favors the pre-flop raiser's range as they are more likely to have an Ace. I have no pair and no draw. Checking is the standard play here to see how the opponent responds. If the opponent bets, I will likely fold given the lack of connectivity my hand has with this board.

On a board of [5h Ad 2h], I have a Jack-high flush draw. As the pre-flop aggressor, the Ace heavily favors my range, making a continuation bet credible. This semi-bluff serves two purposes: it builds the pot for when I complete my flush, and it may fold out better hands like small pocket pairs or weak pairs that missed the Ace. Given the opponent's passive play in previous hands (often checking/calling), a bet of half the pot (200) puts pressure on them while keeping the price reasonable if I need to fold to a massive raise, though calling a standard raise would be viable given my equity.

I have King-high on a board featuring an Ace and a potential flush draw. I missed this flop entirely and have no draws to improve my hand. Pro, as the pre-flop aggressor, is very likely to have an Ace or better in their range. Calling a bet here with nothing but King-high on an Ace-high board is not profitable in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 5-4 suited, which is a solid hand to play aggressively. Given the previous hands, there has been a lot of folding to pre-flop raises. Raising to 300 puts pressure on the big blind and allows me to take control of the hand with position for later streets if called.

I hold Pocket Deuces (2h 2d) in the Big Blind. The opponent (Flash) has raised to 300 (3bb). This is a standard aggressive open from the Button, which they have done consistently. I am getting 2:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 600), requiring roughly 33% equity. Pocket Deuces has roughly 50% equity against a wide Button opening range. Folding a pocket pair heads-up to a single raise is too tight, and 3-betting bloats the pot with a hand that plays poorly post-flop unless it improves to a set. Calling allows me to realize equity and potentially hit a set or evaluate on favorable low boards.

I have flopped bottom set (three deuces) on a board of 2c Jd Kh. This is an incredibly strong hand. The board texture (King, Jack) connects very well with the opponent's pre-flop raising range (Button), meaning they are highly likely to make a continuation bet (c-bet). Leading out (donk betting) would look suspicious and potentially fold out their air or weak hands. By checking, I allow the opponent to fire a c-bet with their top pairs, draws, or bluffs. Once they bet, I can decide whether to check-raise for value or slow play, but the first step is to defer action to the aggressor.

The flop of K-J-2 rainbow favors my pre-flop opening range significantly. Since the opponent checked, I will make a standard continuation bet of 50% pot to represent a strong hand like a King or Jack. This allows me to take down the pot now if the opponent has missed, while also keeping the lead in the hand with position.

I have flopped bottom set on a K-J-2 rainbow board. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range (Broadway cards), so the opponent's c-bet represents either a strong made hand (Top Pair) or a semi-bluff with a gutshot (QT). Because the board is relatively dry (no flush draws), there is little risk in giving a free card. Check-raising here risks folding out the opponent's bluffs or weaker hands that are drawing nearly dead. By just calling, I disguise the strength of my hand, keep their bluffs in the pot, and allow them to continue firing on the turn with top pair, which they will likely view as a value hand. I will call to trap and look to build the pot on later streets.

I hold bottom set on a K-J-2-5 rainbow board. The Turn card (5s) is a blank that changes very little. After check-calling the Flop, leading out now (donk betting) would likely look very strong and could fold out the opponent's bluffs or weaker holdings. The opponent showed aggression on the Flop, so checking again is the best play to induce a second barrel. If the opponent bets, I can consider check-raising to build the pot, but checking first is standard to keep their range wide and allow them to bluff.

On the turn, the 5s gives me bottom pair. While I have some showdown value now, the board (K-J-2-5) still heavily favors my opponent's calling range from the flop (which likely includes Jacks or Kings). By checking back, I control the pot size and avoid a potential check-raise while keeping the pot manageable with a marginal hand. This allows me to see a river card for free and potentially realize my equity.

I hold a set of Deuces on a board of 2c Jd Kh 5s 7d. The pot is 1200. My opponent, Flash, raised pre-flop, c-bet the flop, but checked back the turn. This turn check-back signals that they likely do not hold a monster (top set or two pair would likely bet for value/protection) nor a pure bluff (which might have double-barreled). Their range is likely capped at one-pair hands like Kx or Jx, or potentially a missed gutshot (QT). If I check the river, there is a high probability Flash will check back hands like weak Kings or Jacks to get to showdown cheaply. To maximize value, I must lead out (donk bet) the river. A bet size of roughly 60-65% of the pot (750) is large enough to extract meaningful value from a King, which will have a hard time folding on a blank river, while not being so large that it folds out all marginal hands. Betting guarantees I get money into the pot against their calling range.

On this board of K-J-2-5-7, my pair of fives is only the fourth pair. Looking back at the action, Pro called a continuation bet on the flop (K-J-2), which strongly suggests they have at least a Jack, a King, or a straight draw like Q-T. While the turn check-check may have induced Pro to lead the river as a bluff with a missed draw, my hand is too weak to be a reliable bluff catcher. Most of Pro's calling range on the flop (all Jx and Kx hands) still beats me. Firing a 750 bet into a 1200 pot represents significant strength or a polarized bluff; however, given I have bottom pair, I am at the very bottom of my range. It's better to fold and preserve my stack.

I hold KsQc (KQo) on the Button, which is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. My standard opening strategy has been a min-raise to 200 chips (2BB), as seen in previous hands (#2, #4, #6). Raising builds the pot for value and maintains the initiative while in position. I will continue with the consistent raise size of 200 to disguise the strength of my hand and balance my range.

I am in the Big Blind holding Jh 4h. The opponent in the Small Blind has raised to 200. I need to call 100 more to see the flop, giving me 3:1 pot odds. Suited hands with a Jack have sufficient equity to defend against a standard button opening range in a heads-up match. I will call and see the flop out of position.

I have bottom pair (Jacks) on a very coordinated board (J-Q-K). This board favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. I will check to the opponent to see their action. I don't want to build a pot with a weak kicker and a vulnerable pair.

I have flopped Top Two Pair (Kings and Queens) on a very wet board containing [Jd Qd Kc]. There are significant draws available, including the nut straight (AT), open-ended straight draws (T9), and flush draws (diamonds). While I likely have the best hand currently, giving a free card is dangerous. I need to bet to extract value from worse made hands (like Kx, Qx, two pair counterparts like QJ) and to charge the numerous draws. A half-pot bet of 200 is large enough to charge draws while keeping the opponent's calling range wide enough to include worse hands.

I have bottom pair (Jacks) with a weak kicker on a highly coordinated board (J-Q-K). This board favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, but I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call a half-pot bet. While my hand is vulnerable to many turn cards, folding bottom pair immediately to a single bet in a heads-up match is likely too tight. I will call to see how the turn develops, planning to fold if the aggression continues and I do not improve.

The board has become extremely coordinated with J-Q-K-A. Any Ten now makes a straight. My pair of Jacks is currently the weakest possible pair given the board texture. I am out of position and the Ace is a significant scare card that likely hits my opponent's pre-flop raising range. I will check to see how the opponent acts; if they bet, I will likely have to fold as my hand has almost no showdown value and even hitting a Ten on the river would likely result in a split pot at best.

The turn card is the Ace of Clubs, making the board [Jd, Qd, Kc, Ac]. This puts four cards to a broadway straight on the board; any Ten in the opponent's hand now makes a straight. Given the opponent defended their Big Blind and called a flop bet on a heavy broadway texture, a Ten (like T9, QT, KT, AT, JT) is a very significant part of their range. While I still have Top Two Pair, my hand has been significantly downgraded in relative strength. Betting here is thin for value because better hands (straights, sets of Aces) will continue or raise, and worse hands might fold or simply check-call once. Checking back is the prudent play to exercise pot control, avoid being check-raised, and try to realize equity or improve to a full house on the river for free.

The board is extremely coordinated with four cards to a straight (A-K-Q-J) and three diamonds. My pair of Jacks is the lowest possible pair on this board and has very little showdown value. However, the opponent checked the turn, which may indicate they also find the board texture dangerous or lack a strong hand. I will check the river to reach showdown for free, as betting is unlikely to force a fold from better hands (like any Ace, King, or Queen) and I have some minimal value against total bluffs or missed draws.

The board runout is extremely dangerous, showing [Jd Qd Kc Ac 5d]. This board contains four cards to a broadway straight (any Ten makes a straight) and three diamonds for a possible flush. While I hold Top Two Pair (Kings and Queens), my hand is effectively a bluff-catcher at this point. The opponent check-called the flop and checked the turn and river. While their checks might indicate weakness, they could also be trapping with a straight or flush. More importantly, if I bet, it is highly unlikely that a worse hand (like a weak Ace or lower two pair) calls given the scary board texture, while any Ten, Flush, or better Two Pair will call or raise. Therefore, there is no thin value to be had. Checking back ensures I realize my showdown value against missed draws or weaker pairs without risking more chips.

I am on the button with 7h5h, a suited one-gapper that has excellent post-flop playability. In a heads-up or short-handed scenario, this is a standard open-raising hand to take control of the pot and utilize my positional advantage. I will raise to 300, which is consistent with my previous opening sizes in this tournament.

The opponent has raised to 3BB (300 chips), which creates worse pot odds compared to a standard min-raise. While J4s has some playability due to being suited, it is a weak hand to play out of position against a 3x open. I need to call 200 into a pot that will be 600, requiring 33% raw equity, but my realized equity out of position will likely be lower than the required threshold. Against this sizing, J4s is generally a fold.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with Pocket Threes. In heads-up play, pocket pairs are strong starting hands, generally ahead of the opponent's random range. I have the advantage of position post-flop (acting last). My standard opening strategy has been a min-raise to 200, which puts pressure on the Big Blind and builds the pot slightly without overcommitting with a vulnerable hand. Limping invites the Big Blind to see a free flop with random holdings that could out-flop me. Therefore, I will raise to 200 to maintain initiative and consistency.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Ten offsuit. The opponent has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. Queen-Ten offsuit is a strong hand in a heads-up context and is well ahead of a typical wide opening range from the button. I am getting 3:1 odds on a call (100 to win 300 total pot). While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, folding would be too tight and raising might bloat the pot unnecessarily with a hand that plays well post-flop. I will call to see the flop.

I have hit top pair with a Queen kicker on a fairly coordinated board containing a flush draw and several straight possibilities. Being first to act post-flop, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see their reaction. If the opponent bets, I plan to call to keep the pot manageable while my hand is likely ahead but vulnerable to many turn cards. Checking also allows me to induce bluffs from a wide button opening range.

I hold Pocket Threes (3h 3s) on a wet board of 8h-Ts-6h. Being the pre-flop aggressor in position, I have the option to c-bet. However, this board texture (high connectivity and flush draws) interacts very well with the Big Blind's calling range, which likely contains many pairs (Tx, 8x, 6x), straight draws (97, 75, etc.), or flush draws. My underpair is extremely vulnerable and has little equity if called. Betting turns my hand into a bluff, but I likely won't fold out better hands (like a pair of 6s) or strong draws. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, avoid getting check-raised, and try to get to showdown cheaply or hit a set on the turn. It is the variance-reducing line with a weak showroom value hand on a dynamic board.

I have top pair with a Queen kicker. On the flop, I checked and my opponent checked back. The deuce on the turn is a safe card that doesn't change the board texture significantly. By checking again, I am continuing to control the size of the pot and giving my opponent the opportunity to bluff with their missed draws or air. If the opponent bets, I have a very comfortable call. If it goes check-check again, I will look to value bet most river cards.

The turn brings the 2d, which is a relative blank that doesn't change the board texture significantly. My hand (Pocket Threes) remains an underpair with marginal showdown value. The opponent has checked twice, indicating weakness or a drawing hand. Betting here has limited utility: I am unlikely to get value from worse hands, and better hands (like any pair of 6s, 8s, or 10s) will not fold. Betting only really serves to fold out overcards, but checking back guarantees I see the river for free, keeping the pot small which suits my vulnerable hand strength. I aim to get to showdown as cheaply as possible.

I have top pair with a Queen kicker. The board run-out is relatively safe, although the river 4h completes a potential heart flush. Since the opponent checked both the flop and the turn, it is highly unlikely they are holding a monster hand or even a strong draw they wanted to semi-bluff. I will place a value bet of 200, which is 50% of the pot. This sizing is designed to get called by middle pairs like an 8 or a 6, or a weaker Ten that would have checked back if I checked. If the opponent raises, I will likely fold as a raise after such a passive line usually indicates a completed flush or straight.

The board runout of [8h Ts 6h 2d 4h] is quite coordinated, with three hearts and multiple straight possibilities (e.g., 5-7 makes a straight). My hand, Pocket Threes, is an underpair to almost the entire board (4, 6, 8, T) and only beats a pair of 2s or pure bluffs (Ace-high/King-high). While I checked back the flop and turn to induce action or get to showdown cheaply, the opponent's river lead of 200 (1/3 pot) feels like a thin value bet from a hand like a pair of 4s, 6s, or 8s trying to get called by Ace-high. Given that I lose to any random pair connected to the board and block the straight (holding two 3s makes it less likely they have 5-3, but 5-7 is still possible), my hand is too weak to catch a bluff here. Preserving chips is better than making a low-probability hero call.

I am currently on the button (SB) with 84o, which is a very weak hand. While I have been consistently raising from the button to put pressure on 'Pro', 84o is at the bottom of the range and does not play well post-flop. Given that I have already established an aggressive button image, folding here allows me to maintain some balance and avoid over-committing with trash hands. Since I am slightly behind in chips and have 140 hands remaining, there is no need to force a play with this hand.

I hold J9o on the Button (Small Blind), which is a respectable hand in a Heads-Up format, well within the top 50% of starting hands. My stack is 105 BB effective. Standard strategy dictates opening this hand to take the initiative and utilize position post-flop. I will make a standard minimum raise to 200. This puts pressure on the Big Blind to defend and builds the pot while minimizing risk if 3-bet. Given the opponent has defended his Big Blind frequently but folded to aggression post-flop in previous hands, entering the pot with a raise is the most profitable play.

I am holding Ten-Nine suited in the Big Blind. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 200. With 100 already posted, it costs me only 100 more to see a flop. This provides excellent 3:1 pot odds. T9s is a premium speculative hand that flops well, so calling to see a flop is a standard and profitable play here.

I am out of position on an Ace-high board. While I have a backdoor flush draw with my Ten-Nine of clubs, I do not have a made hand. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most prudent play here to see how they react to this board texture.

I hold J9o on an Ace-high board (Ac 8h 2c). This texture heavily favors my range as the pre-flop aggressor, as I have all the strong Ax hands while the Big Blind defender is capped. In Hand #6, on a very similar board (Ad 5h 2h), my opponent folded to a c-bet of 200. Since I have little showdown value with J high and no draw, betting is the best play to deny equity to random hands and capitalize on my range advantage. A half-pot bet of 200 is consistent with my previous winning line.

I have flopped a flush draw with 9c Tc on a board of 8h Ac 2c. Pro has led out with a half-pot bet of 200 into 400. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, and with a flush draw, my equity is approximately 36% to hit by the river. Calling is the most standard and profitable play here, as it allows me to realize my equity and potentially take the pot down on the turn or river if I improve, while not bloating the pot unnecessarily against a range that likely contains many Aces.

The turn card is the Ace of Spades, pairing the board. I still have a flush draw with my 9c Tc. Since I am out of position and the board has paired the highest card, checking is the most prudent action. This allows me to see if Pro continues their aggression. If Pro checks back, I get a free card to try and hit my flush on the river. If Pro bets, I can evaluate the pot odds to decide whether to continue.

The turn card is the As, pairing the board [Ac 8h 2c As]. The opponent called my flop continuation bet on an Ace-high board. This range usually consists of Ax hands, pairs (like 8x), or flush draws. If the opponent holds an Ace, they now have trips and will certainly not fold to a second bet; they might even check-raise. If they have a flush draw, they will likely call again. A bet here only folds out weak pairs that might be folding anyway, but risks bloating the pot against a very strong range (trips). Checking back is the prudent play to control pot size, avoid being check-raised, and see a river card for free with my J-high hand.

The board has double-paired (Aces and Deuces) with an 8 as the fifth card. My hand is 9c Tc, meaning my best five-card hand is two pair, Aces and Deuces, with a Ten kicker (Ac As 2c 2s Tc). The board's kicker is the 8. Any card in the opponent's hand higher than an 8 (9, T, J, Q, K) will play as the fifth card. Since Pro raised pre-flop and bet the flop, their range includes many hands with high kickers or an Ace. By checking, I am looking for a cheap showdown. Betting is unlikely to get a better hand to fold or a worse hand to call.

I hold Jc9s on a board of Ac 8h 2c As 2s. The board is double-paired (Aces and Deuces) with an 8 kicker. My hand effectively plays as Aces and Deuces with a Jack kicker. The opponent called a flop bet and checked the turn and river. Their range likely consists of an Ace (full house), an 8 (A-A-8-8), a pocket pair (A-A-P-P), or a missed club flush draw. I lose to all pairs and Aces, as well as King/Queen-high missed draws. I only beat lower missed draws (e.g., T-high or lower). Betting is unlikely to fold out a pair or an Ace given my check on the turn, as my line looks like a give-up or a weak hand trying to get to showdown. Betting would only fold out better air (K/Q high), which is a small part of their range compared to the value hands that call. Checking back allows me to win against worse busted draws without risking chips against a range that has me crushed.

Ace-four offsuit is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, typically in the top 25% of hands. Raising to 300 has been my consistent opening size from the button throughout this tournament. This action allows me to maintain initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately, as the opponent has folded to button raises several times in the past few hands.

The opponent (Flash) is opening a very high frequency of buttons (approx 85% observed), consistently using a 3x (300) sizing. Although the larger raise size offers slightly worse pot odds (requiring ~33% equity to call), J9o has robust equity (~48%) against such a wide opening range. Folding here would result in over-folding the Big Blind. The hand is strong enough to defend and evaluate the flop, despite being out of position.

The flop is Qc 4h 6d, a very dry, uncoordinated rainbow board. As the pre-flop caller out of position, I should check almost 100% of my range here to the pre-flop aggressor. My hand (J9o) has no immediate draws and only backdoor potential. Leading out (donking) would accomplish nothing as worse hands (air) would fold and better hands (pairs, better high cards) would call. I will check and evaluate based on whether the opponent decides to c-bet.

On a Queen-high board, my pair of fours with an Ace kicker is currently a middle pair. This is a relatively dry board (Qc 4h 6d), meaning there aren't many immediate draws outside of gutshots like 5-7 or 3-5. Pro checked, which is standard for the big blind after calling a pre-flop raise. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to maintain the initiative, protect my hand against overcards, and extract value from weaker pairs or draws. This also allows me to define the strength of Pro's hand.

The opponent C-bets 300 (half-pot) on a dry Qc 4h 6d board. While J9o had reasonable pre-flop equity against a wide opening range, on this specific texture, it has missed completely, holding only Jack-high with minimal backdoor potential. I am getting 25% pot odds to call, but my equity against a C-betting range (which includes Qx, pocket pairs, sets, and better high cards like Ax/Kx) is poor, likely below the required threshold to float profitably out of position. Furthermore, the half-pot sizing is large enough to deny profitable floating with such a marginal hand. I have many better hands to defend in my range (pairs, gutshots, strong Ax/Kx), so folding the bottom of my range here is standard.

I am on the Button with A5o, which is a strong enough hand to open for a raise in heads-up play. My standard opening size in this match has been a min-raise to 200, which risks little to apply pressure and define ranges. This action allows me to maintain the initiative and play in position post-flop against an opponent who defends their big blind frequently but often folds to aggression on later streets.

I have a strong pocket pair (99) in the Big Blind. The opponent has been consistently opening to 200 from the button. In heads-up play, 99 is a premium hand, and I want to play it aggressively to build value and take the initiative, especially since I will be out of position post-flop. I will 3-bet to 600 to put pressure on the opponent's wide opening range.

I am holding A5o on the Button (SB) and raised to 200. The opponent (Flash) in the BB has 3-bet to 600. This is the first time in 14 hands that Flash has 3-bet my opening raise; previously, they have exclusively called (5 times) or folded (1 time). This sharp deviation from a passive defending strategy strongly suggests a premium value range (likely TT+, AJ+, or better). Against this range, A5o is frequently dominated (kicker trouble) or significantly behind. Although I have position post-flop and get 3:1 pot odds, the reverse implied odds and the likelihood of being up against a much stronger Ace make calling -EV. Preserving my chip lead by folding to this specific show of strength is the disciplined play.

I have Ace-Four offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a standard opening hand. Based on my previous play, I have been consistently raising to 300 from the button to exert pressure and build the pot with superior starting hands. I will continue this pattern here.

I hold Pocket Eights (8s 8d) in the Big Blind facing a 3bb (300) open raise from the Button. In Heads-Up play, 88 is a very strong hand. While a 3-bet is a valid option to take initiative and charge overcards, I am out of position post-flop. The opponent has shown a tendency to open 3x frequently, suggesting a reasonably wide range. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable, keep their range wide (including hands I dominate), and evaluate the flop texture without bloating the pot with a hand that is vulnerable to overcards. I will call to see a flop.

I hold a pair of Eights on a wet board of Tc Jd 3d. There are two overcards to my pair, and the texture connects well with a standard Button opening range (broadways, diamonds). Being out of position, leading out (donk betting) is generally poor as it likely only gets action from better hands or strong draws. Checking is the standard line to control the pot size. I intend to evaluate based on the opponent's action; if they check back, that's a good result for me. If they bet, I will have to decide based on sizing, though this texture is dangerous for 88.

On a board of Tc-Jd-3d, I have Ace-high with the Ace of Diamonds, providing me with a backdoor nut flush draw and an overcard. The board is quite coordinated, connecting with many hands in the opponent's defending range. However, as the pre-flop aggressor, I want to maintain initiative. By betting 300 (half the pot), I can represent a strong pocket pair or a Jack, potentially forcing a fold from the opponent's air or weak draws. If I am called, I still have several ways to improve on the turn.

I hold a pair of Eights (8s 8d) on a flop of Tc Jd 3d. The board contains two overcards to my pair and connects heavily with the Button's opening range, which includes many broadway hands (Tx, Jx, QK, AT) and draws. Although 88 is a strong starting hand heads-up, its value diminishes significantly on this wet texture, especially out of position. Facing a half-pot c-bet (300) and given the high likelihood that the turn will bring a scare card (overcard or diamond), continuing is difficult. Additionally, previous hands suggest the opponent is capable of checking back missed flops or weaker hands, implying their betting range here is weighted towards value or strong equity. Folding prevents bleeding chips in a spot with poor reverse implied odds.

I hold Queen-Ten offsuit (QcTd) on the Button (Small Blind). In heads-up play, this is a strong starting hand, well within the top range of hands to open. My opponent, Flash, has shown a tendency to defend their Big Blind by calling min-raises frequently (Hands 4, 6, 8, 10, 12) but has only 3-bet once (Hand 14). Raising to 200 (min-raise) allows me to build the pot with a good hand while maintaining position post-flop. It is a standard, low-risk play to seize the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind holding 8s 6h. Pro has raised to 200 from the Button. This is a 2x raise, requiring me to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3-to-1 odds. While 86 offsuit is a marginal hand, it is standard to defend the Big Blind with a wide range in heads-up play, especially at these pot odds. I will call to see the flop and see if I can connect with a pair or a draw.

I have completely missed this board with eight-high and no draws. The board (Ace, King, Jack) is extremely favorable for the pre-flop raiser's range, as it contains all the high Broadway cards. Being out of position, I will check and intend to fold to any significant bet, as there is no viable way for my hand to improve or successfully bluff on this texture.

I have flopped the nut straight (Broadway) on a board of Kc-Jd-Ah with my Qc-Td. The board texture is heavy with broadway cards, which connects well with the opponent's defending range (specifically Ax, Kx, Jx, and two-pair combinations). My opponent, Flash, has demonstrated a tendency to play passively post-flop, frequently check-calling bets (e.g., Hand #8, #12). Because I hold the nuts and block the straight draws (Q and T), it is less likely my opponent has a draw, but very likely they have a pair given the high cards. To maximize value against a passive station who calls flop bets but rarely bets themselves, I should build the pot immediately rather than slow-playing and risking the action checking down. I will fire a standard continuation bet.
The flop of Ace-King-Jack is disastrous for my eight-high hand and perfectly hits the pre-flop raiser's range. I have zero equity and no realistic draws. There is no reason to continue in this pot.

In heads-up play, A8o is a strong hand that should be played aggressively from the button. I have established a consistent opening size of 300 in previous hands when in this position, and I will maintain that sizing here to put pressure on the Big Blind and build the pot.

The opponent has raised to 3x (300 chips) from the button, a sizing they have used frequently. While Q3o has decent raw equity against a wide heads-up range, the 3x sizing offers worse pot odds (calling 200 to win 600 total, requiring 33% equity realization) compared to a min-raise. Playing Q3 offsuit out of position is difficult due to poor playability and domination issues (weak kicker). Given the opponent's aggressive c-betting tendency shown in previous hands, realizing equity will be hard. Folding is the disciplined choice.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with K7 suited, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up poker (top ~30-40% of hands). My opponent, Flash, has defended their Big Blind frequently against my standard raises but has only 3-bet once. Raising to 200 allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot pre-flop, or play a pot in position with a hand that has good flush and high-card potential. Folding is too passive for this holding.

I am in the Big Blind with 5h 6h, a solid suited connector in heads-up play. The opponent, Pro, raised to 200 from the Small Blind. I need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 odds. Given our deep stacks and the playability of my hand post-flop, calling is a standard and profitable play to see if I can hit a straight or flush draw.

I have 5-high on a board of 8-K-T. I have no pair and no draw. On this texture, which favors the pre-flop raiser's range, I should check to the aggressor. If a bet comes, I will likely fold as I have no equity to continue.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a weak kicker (7) on a connected board (K-T-8). My opponent, Flash, has demonstrated a 'fit-or-fold' tendency, frequently folding to continuation bets (Hands #4, #6, #13, #15, #16) but calling when they connect (Hands #8, #12). Betting serves two purposes: 1) It extracts value from worse pairs like Tens or Eights and charges draws (J9, QJ); 2) It denies free equity to random hands. Since Flash rarely check-raises, a bet is relatively safe. Checking back is a viable pot-control option, but given Flash's high fold frequency to c-bets, taking down the pot immediately or getting value from weaker hands is the superior line. I will bet half-pot (200).

I am in the Big Blind with 5h 6h on a board of 8s-Kc-Td. I have no pair and no real draw. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range (Pro), which includes many Kings, Tens, and Broadway cards. After Pro bets 200 into a 400 pot, I have no equity to continue and must fold.

I have pocket tens (ThTd) on the button, which is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. Historically, I have been raising to 300 from the button, but against this opponent, I want to build a larger pot or take down the blinds immediately. Pocket tens are significantly ahead of a random big blind range. A standard raise to 300 is consistent with my previous play and provides good value while maintaining control of the pot size.

I am in the Big Blind with [Tc Js] facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button. My hand, Jack-Ten offsuit, is well within the standard defense range for heads-up play, having roughly 50% equity against a wide button opening range. The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 to win a pot of 600, requiring 33% equity). Being out of position with deep stacks (100BB+), a call is preferable to a 3-bet to keep the pot manageable and retain the opponent's wider range. Folding is too tight given the hand strength and odds.

I hold Ten-Jack offsuit on a flop of 6d-8c-Ah. I have missed the flop completely, having no pair and no immediate straight or flush draws. The Ace on the board favors the pre-flop raiser's range significantly more than my calling range. As the out-of-position player, leading out (donk betting) would be unwise as it folds out few better hands and isolates me against stronger holdings. The standard play is to check to the aggressor. If the opponent bets, I will likely fold given my lack of equity, but checking gives me a chance to see a free turn if the opponent checks back.

I hold pocket tens on an Ace-high board. While my hand is strong, the Ace is a significant scare card in the big blind's range, as they will defend many Ace-x hands. Checking the flop allows me to control the pot size and keep their range wide, potentially inducing a bluff on the turn from hands like 79 or smaller pairs that I currently beat. Betting here might only get called by better (Aces) or fold out worse hands that I want to keep in.

The opponent checked back on the Ace-high flop, which usually caps their range to marginal made hands or air, though a trap is possible. The Turn [7s] puts a 4-to-a-straight texture on the board (6-7-8) and gives me a gutshot straight draw (need a 9). While a 'probe' bet here is a valid option to fold out better high cards (KQ/QJ), my hand has some showdown value against total air, and I want to realize my equity for free if possible. I am out of position and prefer not to build a pot with just a gutshot and high card. Checking allows me to see the river cheaply or fold comfortably if faced with significant aggression.

I have pocket tens on a board that is relatively dry but contains an Ace. I checked back the flop to control the pot and under-represent my hand. Pro has now checked for a second time on the turn. While my tens are likely the best hand, an Ace is a significant part of the big blind's defending range. By checking back again, I am continuing to control the pot size and ensuring I get to a cheap showdown, while also potentially inducing a bluff on the river from missed draws or smaller pairs.

I hold the nut straight (Jack-high straight with 7-8-9-T-J) on a board of 6-8-A-7-9. The opponent checked back both the flop and the turn, which strongly suggests they hold a marginal made hand (like a weak Ace or underpair) or air, rather than a strong monster. On this 4-to-a-straight board, an opponent with a marginal hand will almost certainly check back the river to avoid facing a check-raise. Therefore, checking is likely to result in a missed value opportunity. I must bet to extract value. I chose a half-pot sizing (300) which is small enough to induce a call from an Ace or a curious pair getting good odds, but large enough to build the pot significantly.

On a board of 6d-8c-Ah-7s-9s, my pocket tens (ThTd) have improved to the nut straight (6-7-8-9-T). Having checked back both the flop and the turn to control the pot and disguise my hand strength, my range looks relatively weak to my opponent. Pro's lead of 300 into 600 on the river suggests they either have a hand they think is for value (like an Ace or a lower straight with a 5) or they are attempting to take the pot after I showed weakness. Since I hold the nuts, I must raise for value. A raise to 900 (a 600 increase) is a strong value bet that can still be called by top pair or any 5.

I hold the absolute nuts with a Jack-high straight (7-8-9-T-J) on a board of 6-8-A-7-9. The opponent has raised my river lead, which strongly suggests they hold a Ten (making the lower 6-7-8-9-T straight) or are bluffing. Since I beat the lower straight, simply calling would leave significant value on the table. I must 3-bet (re-raise) to extract maximum value from his strong second-best hands. A raise to 2400 is large enough to build the pot significantly while likely keeping a Ten in, as the opponent will find it hard to fold a straight.

On a board of 6d-8c-Ah-7s-9s, my pocket tens (ThTd) hold the current nuts (the best possible hand) with a ten-high straight. After I raised to 900, Pro has three-bet to 2400. This is a very strong action that indicates they likely have a straight themselves, perhaps the 5-high straight (5-6-7-8-9), or they are overplaying a set or two-pair. Since I have the absolute nuts, I have no reason to fold and every reason to continue building the pot. I will call the 2400 for now to maintain the current pot dynamic, or raise further to extract maximum value. Given this is the river and I hold the nuts, I should raise to get the rest of the chips in if possible, but a call is not an option when I have the nuts. I will raise.

I hold the absolute nut straight (Jack-high straight: 7-8-9-T-J) on a board of 6-8-A-7-9. The opponent has 4-bet the river to 3900, which heavily indicates he holds a Ten (making the lower 6-7-8-9-T straight) and believes he has the nuts, or he also holds a Jack for a chop. Given the aggressive action, he is almost certainly committed to the hand. I beat his range (specifically T-x hands). Just calling would leave significant value on the table. I must raise to put him all-in and extract maximum value from his second-best straight. My total all-in amount is my current bet (2400) plus my remaining stack (7750), totaling 10150.

On the river board of 6d-8c-Ah-7s-9s, I have a 10-high straight (10-9-8-7-6) using my pocket tens. The board shows a four-card straight (6-7-8-9). My hand is the second nuts, only losing to a Jack-high straight (J-10-9-8-7). Because I hold two of the tens in the deck (Th and Td), it is mathematically improbable for my opponent to hold both a Jack and one of the two remaining tens. Given the intense betting action on the river, Pro is representing an extremely strong hand, most likely a straight. Pro could easily be holding a single ten (splitting with me), a 5 (a lower 9-high straight), or even overplaying three-of-a-kind or two-pair. In a heads-up scenario, folding a straight when I block the only hand that beats me is not an option. I am getting excellent pot odds to call, and with the 'effective nuts' in this spot, I must commit my remaining stack.
